text: Transvaginal Repair of Anterior Rectocele A Video Technique We used this approach for the treatment of anterior rectocele in thirty seven years old female patient with the history of three vaginal deliveries. The last one was in two thousand and six. The patient also had a history of hemorrhoidectomy ten years ago. Manometric study showed that the resting pressure was fifty six millimeters mercury. while the squeeze pressure was one hundred and forty two millimeters mercury, and the rectoenol inhibitory reflex was intact. Dynamic defecography revealed a five point nine centimeters anterior rectocele.
Operative setting. No preoperative bowel preparation. The patient received antibiotic prophylaxis in form of intravenous administration of one gram second generation cephalosporin.

The patient is positioned in a modified lithotomy position under spinal anesthesia. The surgeon assesses the size and extent of the anterior rectocele before repair, and injects adrenalized saline to reduce bleeding and facilitate dissection.

The surgeon makes an incision in the vaginal mucosa starting at the apex of the rectocele and extending distally to the posterior fornix. Dissection is performed in the proper plane to separate the mucosa from the underlying rectovaginal septum.

The surgeon marks and excises the excess vaginal mucosa. The repair is performed in two layers, starting with the plication of the rectovaginal septum using interrupted 2-0 absorbable sutures placed proximally, in the middle, and distally near the posterior forchead.
The surgeon places three interrupted 2-0 absorbable sutures over the first layer of repair, followed by closure of the mucosal incision using interrupted 2-0 absorbable sutures.

The appearance after the repair. The patient was allowed to start oral intake on the same day of operation. The patient was discharged on the second postoperative day. The use of laxatives were recommended for six weeks. The patient was instructed to defer sexual intercourse for six weeks.
